op.name: loadtxt
op.notes: Load data from text file. Not supported.
op.summary: Load data from a text file.
op.signature: we.loadtxt(fname, dtype=<class 'float'>, comments='#', delimiter=None, converters=None, skiprows=0, usecols=None, unpack=False, ndmin=0, encoding=None, max_rows=None, *, quotechar=None, like=None)

blocks.text: Load data from a text file.

items.name: fname
items.type: file, str, pathlib.Path, list of str, generator

desc_blocks.text:
File, filename, list, or generator to read.  If the filename
extension is ``.gz`` or ``.bz2``, the file is first decompressed. Note
that generators must return bytes or strings. The strings
in a list or produced by a generator are treated as lines.

Data-type of the resulting array; default: float.  If this is a
structured data-type, the resulting array will be 1-dimensional, and
each row will be interpreted as an element of the array.  In this
case, the number of columns used must match the number of fields in
the data-type.

items.name: comments
items.type: str or sequence of str or None, optional

desc_blocks.text:
The characters or list of characters used to indicate the start of a
comment. None implies no comments. For backwards compatibility, byte
strings will be decoded as 'latin1'. The default is '#'.

The character used to separate the values. For backwards compatibility,
byte strings will be decoded as 'latin1'. The default is whitespace.

desc_blocks.name: versionchanged:: 1.23.0
desc_blocks.body:
Only single character delimiters are supported. Newline characters
   cannot be used as the delimiter.
desc_blocks.raw:
.. versionchanged:: 1.23.0
   Only single character delimiters are supported. Newline characters
   cannot be used as the delimiter.

desc_blocks.text:
Converter functions to customize value parsing. If `converters` is
callable, the function is applied to all columns, else it must be a
dict that maps column number to a parser function.
See examples for further details.
Default: None.

desc_blocks.name: versionchanged:: 1.23.0
desc_blocks.body:
The ability to pass a single callable to be applied to all columns
   was added.
desc_blocks.raw:
.. versionchanged:: 1.23.0
   The ability to pass a single callable to be applied to all columns
   was added.

desc_blocks.text: Skip the first `skiprows` lines, including comments; default: 0.

Which columns to read, with 0 being the first. For example,
``usecols = (1,4,5)`` will extract the 2nd, 5th and 6th columns.
The default, None, results in all columns being read.

If True, the returned array is transposed, so that arguments may be
unpacked using ``x, y, z = loadtxt(...)``.  When used with a
structured data-type, arrays are returned for each field.
Default is False.

The returned array will have at least `ndmin` dimensions.
Otherwise mono-dimensional axes will be squeezed.
Legal values: 0 (default), 1 or 2.

Encoding used to decode the inputfile. Does not apply to input streams.
The special value 'bytes' enables backward compatibility workarounds
that ensures you receive byte arrays as results if possible and passes
'latin1' encoded strings to converters. Override this value to receive
unicode arrays and pass strings as input to converters.  If set to None
the system default is used. The default value is 'bytes'.

desc_blocks.name: versionchanged:: 2.0
desc_blocks.body:
Before NumPy 2, the default was ``'bytes'`` for Python 2
    compatibility. The default is now ``None``.
desc_blocks.raw:
.. versionchanged:: 2.0
    Before NumPy 2, the default was ``'bytes'`` for Python 2
    compatibility. The default is now ``None``.

desc_blocks.text:
Read `max_rows` rows of content after `skiprows` lines. The default is
to read all the rows. Note that empty rows containing no data such as
empty lines and comment lines are not counted towards `max_rows`,
while such lines are counted in `skiprows`.

desc_blocks.name: versionchanged:: 1.23.0
desc_blocks.body:
Lines containing no data, including comment lines (e.g., lines
    starting with '#' or as specified via `comments`) are not counted
    towards `max_rows`.
desc_blocks.raw:
.. versionchanged:: 1.23.0
    Lines containing no data, including comment lines (e.g., lines
    starting with '#' or as specified via `comments`) are not counted
    towards `max_rows`.

desc_blocks.text:
The character used to denote the start and end of a quoted item.
Occurrences of the delimiter or comment characters are ignored within
a quoted item. The default value is ``quotechar=None``, which means
quoting support is disabled.

If two consecutive instances of `quotechar` are found within a quoted
field, the first is treated as an escape character. See examples.

Reference object to allow the creation of arrays which are not
    NumPy arrays. If an array-like passed in as ``like`` supports
    the ``__array_function__`` protocol, the result will be defined
    by it. In this case, it ensures the creation of an array object
    compatible with that passed in via this argument.
Data read from the text file.

blocks.text:
This function aims to be a fast reader for simply formatted files.  The
`genfromtxt` function provides more sophisticated handling of, e.g.,
lines with missing values.

Each row in the input text file must have the same number of values to be
able to read all values. If all rows do not have same number of values, a
subset of up to n columns (where n is the least number of values present
in all rows) can be read by specifying the columns via `usecols`.

The strings produced by the Python float.hex method can be used as
input for floats.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> from io import StringIO   # StringIO behaves like a file object
>>> c = StringIO("0 1\n2 3")
>>> np.loadtxt(c)
array([[0., 1.],
       [2., 3.]])

>>> d = StringIO("M 21 72\nF 35 58")
>>> np.loadtxt(d, dtype={'names': ('gender', 'age', 'weight'),
...                      'formats': ('S1', 'i4', 'f4')})
array([(b'M', 21, 72.), (b'F', 35, 58.)],
      dtype=[('gender', 'S1'), ('age', '<i4'), ('weight', '<f4')])

>>> c = StringIO("1,0,2\n3,0,4")
>>> x, y = np.loadtxt(c, delimiter=',', usecols=(0, 2), unpack=True)
>>> x
array([1., 3.])
>>> y
array([2., 4.])

blocks.text:
The `converters` argument is used to specify functions to preprocess the
text prior to parsing. `converters` can be a dictionary that maps
preprocessing functions to each column:

blocks.code:
>>> s = StringIO("1.618, 2.296\n3.141, 4.669\n")
>>> conv = {
...     0: lambda x: np.floor(float(x)),  # conversion fn for column 0
...     1: lambda x: np.ceil(float(x)),  # conversion fn for column 1
... }
>>> np.loadtxt(s, delimiter=",", converters=conv)
array([[1., 3.],
       [3., 5.]])

blocks.text:
`converters` can be a callable instead of a dictionary, in which case it
is applied to all columns:

blocks.code:
>>> s = StringIO("0xDE 0xAD\n0xC0 0xDE")
>>> import functools
>>> conv = functools.partial(int, base=16)
>>> np.loadtxt(s, converters=conv)
array([[222., 173.],
       [192., 222.]])

blocks.text:
This example shows how `converters` can be used to convert a field
with a trailing minus sign into a negative number.

blocks.code:
>>> s = StringIO("10.01 31.25-\n19.22 64.31\n17.57- 63.94")
>>> def conv(fld):
...     return -float(fld[:-1]) if fld.endswith("-") else float(fld)
...
>>> np.loadtxt(s, converters=conv)
array([[ 10.01, -31.25],
       [ 19.22,  64.31],
       [-17.57,  63.94]])

blocks.text:
Using a callable as the converter can be particularly useful for handling
values with different formatting, e.g. floats with underscores:

This idea can be extended to automatically handle values specified in
many different formats, such as hex values:

blocks.code:
>>> def conv(val):
...     try:
...         return float(val)
...     except ValueError:
...         return float.fromhex(val)
>>> s = StringIO("1, 2.5, 3_000, 0b4, 0x1.4000000000000p+2")
>>> np.loadtxt(s, delimiter=",", converters=conv)
array([1.0e+00, 2.5e+00, 3.0e+03, 1.8e+02, 5.0e+00])

blocks.text: Or a format where the ``-`` sign comes after the number:

Support for quoted fields is enabled with the `quotechar` parameter.
Comment and delimiter characters are ignored when they appear within a
quoted item delineated by `quotechar`:

blocks.code:
>>> s = StringIO('"alpha, #42", 10.0\n"beta, #64", 2.0\n')
>>> dtype = np.dtype([("label", "U12"), ("value", float)])
>>> np.loadtxt(s, dtype=dtype, delimiter=",", quotechar='"')
array([('alpha, #42', 10.), ('beta, #64',  2.)],
      dtype=[('label', '<U12'), ('value', '<f8')])

blocks.text: Quoted fields can be separated by multiple whitespace characters:

blocks.code:
>>> s = StringIO('"alpha, #42"       10.0\n"beta, #64" 2.0\n')
>>> dtype = np.dtype([("label", "U12"), ("value", float)])
>>> np.loadtxt(s, dtype=dtype, delimiter=None, quotechar='"')
array([('alpha, #42', 10.), ('beta, #64',  2.)],
      dtype=[('label', '<U12'), ('value', '<f8')])

blocks.text:
Two consecutive quote characters within a quoted field are treated as a
single escaped character:

blocks.code:
>>> s = StringIO('"Hello, my name is ""Monty""!"')
>>> np.loadtxt(s, dtype="U", delimiter=",", quotechar='"')
array('Hello, my name is "Monty"!', dtype='<U26')

blocks.text: Read subset of columns when all rows do not contain equal number of values:
genfromtxt — Load data with missing values handled as specified.
